perturbation.original_text: Company shall be responsible for all costs relating to the development of the Recipes and Deliverables, except for costs and expenses relating to the involvement of Reed's personnel and contractors.
perturbation.changed_text: Company shall be responsible for all costs relating to the development of the Recipes and Deliverables, including costs and expenses relating to the involvement of Reed's personnel and contractors, up to $10,000. Any costs exceeding $10,000 relating to Reed's personnel will be the responsibility of Reed's.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces a contradiction regarding who is financially responsible for Reed's personnel costs. Section 2 originally states Company is *not* responsible, whereas the modification states Company *is* responsible up to a certain threshold. This contradicts the original statement, leading to ambiguity in cost allocation. This perturbation creates an in-text contradiction, with the later added statement contradicting the former. The initial statement provides a straightforward allocation of cost, shifting all expenses related to Reed's personnel and contractors to Reed's. The perturbation adjusts this arrangement by assigning a limited financial responsibility to the Company for those same expenses
perturbation.contradicted_text: Consideration. In exchange for Company's contributions and obligations under this Agreement, Reed's grants Company the exclusive right to manufacture, package, promote, sell and distribute the Products (if and to the extent approved by the Development Committee), subject to the terms and conditions of a separate Manufacturing and Distribution Agreement to be entered into by the parties concurrently with this Agreement, as it may be amended, modified, supplemented or restated from time to time (the "Manufacturing and Distribution Agreement"). Company shall be responsible for all costs relating to the development of the Recipes and Deliverables, including costs and expenses relating to the involvement of Reed's personnel and contractors, up to $10,000. Any costs exceeding $10,000 relating to Reed's personnel will be the responsibility of Reed's.
perturbation.justification: The original text stated the Company was *not* responsible for costs related to Reed's personnel. The changed text explicitly states the Company *is* responsible for those costs up to $10,000. This creates a direct contradiction in the financial responsibilities of the parties, leading to ambiguity and potential disputes, directly fitting the definition of 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: Reed's will exclusively own all Deliverables. Company will and hereby does, without further consideration, irrevocably assign to Reed's any and all worldwide right, title or interest that Company may now or hereafter possess in or to the Deliverables in perpetuity (or the maximum period permitted by Applicable Laws and Regulations) and Reed's accepts such assignment. Company will execute and deliver documents reasonably requested by Reed's to register its Intellectual Property in the Deliverables.
perturbation.changed_text: Reed's will exclusively own all Deliverables, except for any Deliverables directly incorporating Company Intellectual Property. Company will and hereby does, without further consideration, irrevocably assign to Reed's any and all worldwide right, title or interest that Company may now or hereafter possess in or to the Deliverables in perpetuity (or the maximum period permitted by Applicable Laws and Regulations) and Reed's accepts such assignment. Company will execute and deliver documents reasonably requested by Reed's to register its Intellectual Property in the Deliverables.
perturbation.explanation: The original text unequivocally states that Reed's owns *all* Deliverables. The modified text creates an exception, stating that Deliverables that incorporate Company Intellectual Property are *not* exclusively owned by Reed's. This creates an in-text contradiction in section 6(a)(i) regarding ownership rights. The perturbation contradicts the initial assignment statement within the same section, leading to confusion about the scope of ownership.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Intellectual Property. (a) Ownership of Intellectual Property. (i) Company will create and provide to Reed's the Deliverables. Reed's will exclusively own all Deliverables, except for any Deliverables directly incorporating Company Intellectual Property. Company will and hereby does, without further consideration, irrevocably assign to Reed's any and all worldwide right, title or interest that Company may now or hereafter possess in or to the Deliverables in perpetuity (or the maximum period permitted by Applicable Laws and Regulations) and Reed's accepts such assignment. Company will execute and deliver documents reasonably requested by Reed's to register its Intellectual Property in the Deliverables.
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces an exception to the exclusive ownership of deliverables by Reed's, specifically for deliverables incorporating Company Intellectual Property. This directly contradicts the original statement in the same section that Reed's will exclusively own *all* Deliverables. The introduction of this exception creates a clear and direct conflict regarding ownership rights, leading to uncertainty and potential disputes over which party owns the deliverables that incorporate Company IP, and thus falls under the definition of 'Ambiguities - In Text Contradiction'. This is a strong contradiction that would cause confusion and potentially unenforceability.

perturbation.original_text: The Term may be extended by written agreement of the parties.
perturbation.changed_text: The Term may be extended by written agreement of the parties, provided that any such extension shall not exceed one year.
perturbation.explanation: Section 8(a) establishes the initial term and allows extensions via written agreement. The change limits the duration of these extensions to a maximum of one year. This potentially conflicts with other sections (such as Force Majeure) that might require or imply longer extensions. This change creates an in-text contradiction. If a Force Majeure event (Section 15) lasts longer than one year, the limitation in Section 8(a) would prevent the contract from being extended to accommodate it, potentially leading to breach of contract disputes.
perturbation.contradicted_text: Force Majeure. No party shall be liable or responsible to the other party, nor be deemed to have defaulted under or breached this Agreement, for any failure or delay in fulfilling or performing any term of this Agreement (except for any obligations to make payments to the other party hereunder), when and to the extent such failure or delay is caused by or results from the following force majeure events ("Force Majeure Events"): (a) acts of God; (b) flood, fire, earthquake or explosion; (c) war, invasion, hostilities (whether war is declared or not), terrorist threats or acts, riot or other civil unrest; (d) government order or law; (e) actions, embargoes or blockades in effect on or after the date of this Agreement; (f) action by any governmental authority; (g) national or regional emergency; (h) strikes, labor stoppages or slowdowns or other industrial disturbances; (i) shortage of adequate power or transportation facilities; and (j) other similar events beyond the reasonable control of the party impacted by the Force Majeure Event (the "Impacted Party"). The Impacted Party shall give notice within seven (7) days of the Force Majeure Event to the other party, stating the period of time the occurrence is expected to continue. The Impacted Party shall use diligent efforts to end the failure or delay and ensure the effects of such Force Majeure Event are minimized. The Impacted Party shall resume the performance of its obligations as soon as reasonably practicable and to the greatest extent possible after the removal of the cause. In the event that the Impacted Party's failure or delay remains uncured for a period of thirty (30) days following written notice given by it under this Section 15, either party may thereafter terminate this Agreement upon thirty (30) days' written notice.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 8(a) limits contract extensions to one year. Section 15, Force Majeure, allows for contract termination if a force majeure event remains uncured for 30 days after notice. However, a force majeure event could reasonably last longer than one year. The limitation on extensions directly contradicts the possibility of needing a longer extension due to a force majeure event, creating an in-text contradiction as it could lead to a breach of contract situation if Section 8(a) cannot accommodate a force majeure event under Section 15. This ambiguity makes the obligations and rights of each party uncertain and difficult to enforce.
